name: Deer Park Sarnath
description: Deer Park, Sarnath, located near Varanasi in Uttar Pradesh, India, holds immense significance in Buddhist mythology as the site where Gautama Buddha first taught the Dharma after attaining enlightenment. It is considered one of the four holiest places for Buddhists, the others being Lumbini, Bodh Gaya, and Kushinagar. The park was a tranquil sanctuary for deer and other animals, hence its name, providing a fitting environment for the Buddha to deliver his first sermon, the Dhammacakkappavattana Sutta, which outlined the Four Noble Truths and the Eightfold Path. This event marked the beginning of the Sangha, the Buddhist monastic community, with the first five disciples joining the Buddha: Kondanna, Bhaddiya, Vappa, Mahanama, and Assaji. The Ashoka Pillar, erected by Emperor Ashoka in the 3rd century BCE, further commemorates the importance of Sarnath and the spread of Buddhism. The ruins of monasteries and stupas within the park are tangible reminders of its historical and spiritual significance, drawing pilgrims and scholars from around the world to reflect on the Buddha's teachings and the origins of Buddhism. The Dhamek Stupa, a massive stone structure, is believed to mark the spot where the Buddha delivered his first sermon, solidifying Sarnath's place as a cornerstone of Buddhist tradition and a symbol of the Dharma's initial propagation. The park represents the turning of the Wheel of Dharma and the birth of the Buddhist faith.
subtitle: First Sermon: Turning the Wheel of Dharma

keyMyths.title: The First Sermon
keyMyths.description: Gautama Buddha delivered his first sermon, the Dhammacakkappavattana Sutta, outlining the Four Noble Truths and the Eightfold Path. This pivotal moment established the foundation of Buddhist teachings.

keyMyths.title: The First Disciples
keyMyths.description: The Buddha's first five disciples, Kondanna, Bhaddiya, Vappa, Mahanama, and Assaji, joined him in Sarnath, forming the initial Sangha. This marked the beginning of the Buddhist monastic community.

sources.author: T.W. Rhys Davids
sources.description: A comprehensive overview of early Buddhist history and culture in India.
